flashcards.front: What is exception handling in C++?
flashcards.back: A mechanism to handle runtime errors gracefully by separating error detection from error handling using try-catch blocks.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the 'try' block?
flashcards.back: The try block contains code that might throw an exception. If an exception occurs, it transfers control to the appropriate catch block.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the 'catch' block?
flashcards.back: The catch block catches and handles exceptions thrown in the preceding try block. It receives the exception object.
The throw keyword creates and signals that an exceptional condition has occurred, transferring control to the nearest matching catch block.
A catch-all handler that catches any type of exception. It must be the last catch block after specific exception handlers.

flashcards.front: What is stack unwinding in exception handling?
flashcards.back: The process where the runtime destroys local objects in scope as the exception propagates up the call stack, looking for a catch handler.
Using 'throw;' (without an exception object) inside a catch block to pass the caught exception to an outer try-catch block.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between catch by value and catch by reference?
flashcards.back: Catch by value creates a copy, catch by reference avoids copying and preserves polymorphism. Always prefer catch by reference.
The base class for all standard exceptions in C++. It provides what() method to return error message as C-string.

flashcards.front: Name three standard exception classes in C++.
flashcards.back: std::bad_alloc (new fails), std::runtime_error, std::logic_error, std::out_of_range, std::invalid_argument.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of 'noexcept' specifier?
flashcards.back: Declares that a function will not throw exceptions. If an exception escapes a noexcept function, std::terminate is called.
Yes, constructors can throw exceptions. When a constructor throws, the object is not created and destructor is not called for that object.
No, destructors should never throw exceptions. If a destructor throws during stack unwinding, std::terminate is called.

flashcards.front: What is exception specification in C++?
flashcards.back: A deprecated feature that specifies which exceptions a function can throw. Use noexcept instead in modern C++.

flashcards.front: What is the order of catch blocks important?
flashcards.back: More specific exception types should come before more general ones. Derived classes must be caught before base classes.
